channel.title: Philosophy on Julius Broomfield
channel.description: Recent content in Philosophy on Julius Broomfield

item.title: Future Essay Title
item.description: This writing is currently under construction.

item.title: Hermeneutics and Hyperreality: How Recommender Algorithms Mediate Our Perception of the Internet
item.description: The modern internet is too vast to be browsed raw. To make it navigable, we rely on feed algorithms, search rankings, and personalized recommenders. In doing so, we delegate our attention to code. Jean Baudrillard defined hyperreality as a condition in which what is real and what is fiction are seamlessly blended together so that there is no clear distinction for the spectator. Recommender systems do not simply sort information; they curate a hyperreal copy of human interest.

item.title: A Comparative Review of Computational and Human Creativity
item.description: Can computers create? The immediate answer depends entirely on how we define “create.” Historically, human creativity has been mystified—attributed to divine inspiration, subconscious genius, or sudden neuro-chemical leaps. When we evaluate computational systems (such as GANs, Transformers, or diffusion models), we often find ourselves moving the goalposts. If an AI writes a sonnet, we argue it lacks “intent” or “feeling.” If it paints, we claim it is merely interpolating a high-dimensional manifold of training data.

item.title: Memories as an Escape of Schopenhauer's Will
item.description: In The World as Will and Representation, Arthur Schopenhauer describes the “Will” as an aimless, insatiable force that drives all of existence. Because we are expressions of this Will, we are trapped in a cycle of wanting, briefly satisfying a desire, feeling boredom, and desiring again. For Schopenhauer, life is a pendulum swinging between pain and boredom. He offered only a few escapes: Aesthetic Contemplation: Looking at art, particularly music, where we perceive the “pure Ideas” of the world and forget our individual desires.
